content:
# Genesis Chapter 24 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 13r–14r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_024_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_024_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_024_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 19–21 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0023

---

*The Oath and the Commission — Abraham's Charge to His Servant (vv. 1–9)*

**24:1** And Abraham [וְאַבְרָהָם] was old [זָקֵן] — advanced in days [בָּא בַּיָּמִים] — and the LORD [וַיהוָה] had blessed [בֵּרַךְ] Abraham [אֶת-אַבְרָהָם] in all things [בַּכֹּל].

**24:2** And Abraham said [וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם] to his servant [אֶל-עַבְדּוֹ] — the elder [זְקַן] of his house [בֵּיתוֹ] — the one ruling over all [הַמֹּשֵׁל בְּכָל] that was his [אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ]: Put now [שִׂים-נָא] your hand [יָדְךָ] under my thigh [תַּחַת יְרֵכִי] [*tachat yerekhi*].

**24:3** And I will make you swear [וְאַשְׁבִּיעֲךָ] by the LORD [בַּיהוָה] — the God of heaven [אֱלֹהֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם] and the God of the earth [וֵאלֹהֵי הָאָרֶץ] — that you will not take [אֲשֶׁר לֹא-תִקַּח] a wife [אִשָּׁה] for my son [לִבְנִי] from the daughters [מִבְּנוֹת] of the Canaanite [הַכְּנַעֲנִי] in whose midst [אֲשֶׁר אָנֹכִי] I am dwelling [יוֹשֵׁב] in his midst [בְּקִרְבּוֹ].

**24:4** But to my land [כִּי אֶל-אַרְצִי] and to my kindred [וְאֶל-מוֹלַדְתִּי] you shall go [תֵּלֵךְ] and you shall take [וְלָקַחְתָּ] a wife [אִשָּׁה] for my son [לִבְנִי] — for Isaac [לְיִצְחָק].

**24:5** And the servant said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו הָעֶבֶד] to him: Perhaps the woman [אוּלַי לֹא-תֹאבֶה הָאִשָּׁה] will not be willing [לֹא-תֹאבֶה] to go [לָלֶכֶת] after me [אַחֲרַי] to this land [אֶל-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת] — must I indeed return [הֶהָשֵׁב אָשִׁיב] your son [אֶת-בִּנְךָ] to the land [אֶל-הָאָרֶץ] from which you came out [אֲשֶׁר-יָצָאתָ מִשָּׁם]?

**24:6** And Abraham said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו אַבְרָהָם] to him: Guard yourself [הִשָּׁמֶר לְךָ] lest you return [פֶּן-תָּשִׁיב] my son [אֶת-בְּנִי] there [שָׁמָּה].

**24:7** The LORD [יְהוָה] — the God of heaven [אֱלֹהֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם] — who took me [אֲשֶׁר לְקָחַנִי] from the house of my father [מִבֵּית אָבִי] and from the land of my birth [וּמֵאֶרֶץ מוֹלַדְתִּי] and who spoke to me [וַאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר-לִי] and who swore to me [וַאֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּע-לִי] saying [לֵאמֹר]: To your offspring [לְזַרְעֲךָ] I will give [אֶתֵּן] this land [אֶת-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת] — he [הוּא] will send [יִשְׁלַח] his angel [מַלְאָכוֹ] before you [לְפָנֶיךָ] and you shall take [וְלָקַחְתָּ] a wife [אִשָּׁה] for my son [לִבְנִי] from there [מִשָּׁם].

**24:8** And if the woman [וְאִם-לֹא תֹאבֶה הָאִשָּׁה] is not willing [לֹא תֹאבֶה] to go [לָלֶכֶת] after you [אַחֲרֶיךָ] — then you are free [וְנִקִּיתָ] from this oath of mine [מִשְּׁבֻעָתִי זֹאת] — only [רַק] my son [אֶת-בְּנִי] you shall not return [לֹא תָשֵׁב] there [שָׁמָּה].

**24:9** And the servant [וַיָּשֶׂם הָעֶבֶד] put [וַיָּשֶׂם] his hand [אֶת-יָדוֹ] under the thigh [תַּחַת יֶרֶךְ] of Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] his master [אֲדֹנָיו] and he swore to him [וַיִּשָּׁבַע לוֹ] upon this matter [עַל-הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה].

---

*The Servant at the Well — Aram-naharaim (vv. 10–14)*

**24:10** And the servant took [וַיִּקַּח הָעֶבֶד] ten camels [עֲשָׂרָה גְמַלִּים] from the camels [מִגְּמַלֵּי] of his master [אֲדֹנָיו] and he went [וַיֵּלֶךְ] — and all the good [וְכָל-טוּב] of his master [אֲדֹנָיו] was in his hand [בְּיָדוֹ] — and he arose [וַיָּקָם] and went [וַיֵּלֶךְ] to Aram-naharaim [אֶל-אֲרַם נַהֲרַיִם] — to the city [אֶל-עִיר] of Nahor [נָחוֹר].

**24:11** And he made the camels kneel [וַיַּבְרֵךְ הַגְּמַלִּים] outside [מִחוּץ] the city [לָעִיר] at the water well [אֶל-בְּאֵר הַמַּיִם] at the time of evening [לְעֵת עֶרֶב] — the time [לְעֵת] the water-drawers go out [צֵאת הַשֹּׁאֲבֹת].

**24:12** And he said [וַיֹּאמַר]: O LORD [יְהוָה] — God of my master [אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] — cause to happen [הַקְרֵה-נָא] before me [לְפָנַי] today [הַיּוֹם] — and do covenant-faithfulness [וַעֲשֵׂה-חֶסֶד] with my master [עִם-אֲדֹנִי] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם].

**24:13** Behold [הִנֵּה] I am standing [אָנֹכִי נִצָּב] by the spring of water [עַל-עֵין הַמָּיִם] and the daughters [וּבְנוֹת] of the men of the city [אַנְשֵׁי הָעִיר] are going out [יֹצְאֹת] to draw water [לִשְׁאֹב מָיִם].

**24:14** And let it be [וְהָיָה] that the young woman [הַנַּעֲרָ] to whom I say [אֲשֶׁר אֹמַר אֵלֶיהָ]: Incline now [הַטִּי-נָא] your jar [כַּדֵּךְ] and let me drink [וְאֶשְׁתֶּה] — and she says [וְאָמְרָה]: Drink [שְׁתֵה] and also [וְגַם] your camels [גְּמַלֶּיךָ] I will water [אַשְׁקֶה] — she [it is who] you have appointed [אֹתָהּ הֹכַחְתָּ] for your servant [לְעַבְדְּךָ] — for Isaac [לְיִצְחָק] — and by this [וּבָהּ] I will know [אֵדַע] that you have done covenant-faithfulness [כִּי-עָשִׂיתָ חֶסֶד] with my master [עִם-אֲדֹנִי].

---

*Rebekah Appears — The Sign Fulfilled (vv. 15–21)*

**24:15** And it happened [וַיְהִי] — he had not yet finished [טֶרֶם כִּלָּה] speaking [לְדַבֵּר] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] Rebekah [רִבְקָה] was going out [יֹצֵאת] — who was born [אֲשֶׁר יֻלְּדָה] to Bethuel [לִבְתוּאֵל] the son of Milcah [בֶּן-מִלְכָּה] the wife of Nahor [אֵשֶׁת נָחוֹר] the brother of Abraham [אֲחִי אַבְרָהָם] — and her jar [וְכַדָּהּ] was upon her shoulder [עַל-שִׁכְמָהּ].

**24:16** And the young woman [וְהַנַּעֲרָ] was very [מְאֹד] good [טֹבַת] of appearance [מַרְאֶה] — a virgin [בְּתוּלָה] — and no man [וְאִישׁ] had known her [לֹא יְדָעָהּ] — and she went down [וַתֵּרֶד] to the spring [הָעַיְנָה] and she filled [וַתְּמַלֵּא] her jar [כַּדָּהּ] and she came up [וַתַּעַל].

**24:17** And the servant ran [וַיָּרָץ הָעֶבֶד] to meet her [לִקְרָאתָהּ] and he said [וַיֹּאמַר]: Let me drink now [הַגְמִיאִינִי נָא] a little water [מְעַט-מַיִם] from your jar [מִכַּדֵּךְ].

**24:18** And she said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: Drink [שְׁתֵה] my lord [אֲדֹנִי] — and she hurried [וַתְּמַהֵר] and lowered [וַתּוֹרֶד] her jar [כַּדָּהּ] upon her hand [עַל-יָדָהּ] and gave him drink [וַתַּשְׁקֵהוּ].

**24:19** And she finished [וַתְּכַל] giving him drink [לְהַשְׁקֹתוֹ] and she said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: Also for your camels [גַּם לִגְמַלֶּיךָ] I will draw [אֶשְׁאָב] until [עַד] they have finished [אִם-כִּלּוּ] drinking [לִשְׁתֹּת].

**24:20** And she hurried [וַתְּמַהֵר] and emptied [וַתְּעַר] her jar [כַּדָּהּ] into the trough [אֶל-הַשֹּׁקֶת] and she ran again [וַתָּרָץ עוֹד] to the well [אֶל-הַבְּאֵר] to draw [לִשְׁאֹב] and she drew [וַתִּשְׁאַב] for all his camels [לְכָל-גְּמַלָּיו].

**24:21** And the man [וְהָאִישׁ] was gazing at her in silence [מִשְׁתָּאֵה לָהּ מַחֲרִישׁ] [*mishtaeh*] — watching to know [לָדַעַת] whether [הַהִצְלִיחַ] the LORD [יְהוָה] had made prosperous [הִצְלִיחַ] his way [דַּרְכּוֹ] or not [אִם-לֹא].

---

*The Gifts and the Question — Who Are You? (vv. 22–27)*

**24:22** And it happened [וַיְהִי] when the camels had finished [כַּאֲשֶׁר כִּלּוּ הַגְּמַלִּים] drinking [לִשְׁתּוֹת] — and the man took [וַיִּקַּח הָאִישׁ] a gold nose-ring [נֶזֶם זָהָב] — its weight a half-shekel [בֶּקַע מִשְׁקָלוֹ] — and two bracelets [וּשְׁנֵי צְמִידִים] upon her hands [עַל-יָדֶיהָ] — ten [gold] [עֲשָׂרָה] of gold [זָהָב] was their weight [מִשְׁקָלָם].

**24:23** And he said [וַיֹּאמַר]: Whose daughter [בַּת-מִי] are you [אַתְּ]? Tell me now [הַגִּידִי-נָא לִי] — is there [הֲיֵשׁ] in the house of your father [בֵּית-אָבִיךְ] a place [מָקוֹם] for us to lodge [לָנוּ לָלִין]?

**24:24** And she said [וַתֹּאמֶר] to him: I am the daughter [בַּת-בְּתוּאֵל] of Bethuel [בְּתוּאֵל] — the son of Milcah [בֶּן-מִלְכָּה] — whom she bore [אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה] to Nahor [לְנָחוֹר].

**24:25** And she said [וַתֹּאמֶר] to him: Both straw [גַּם-תֶּבֶן] and also feed [גַּם-מִסְפּוֹא] is plentiful [רַב] with us [עִמָּנוּ] — and also [גַּם] a place [מָקוֹם] to lodge [לָלוּן].

**24:26** And the man bowed down [וַיִּקֹּד הָאִישׁ] and prostrated himself [וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ] before the LORD [לַיהוָה].

**24:27** And he said [וַיֹּאמַר]: Blessed [בָּרוּךְ] be the LORD [יְהוָה] — the God of my master [אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] — who has not forsaken [אֲשֶׁר לֹא-עָזַב] his covenant-faithfulness [חַסְדּוֹ] and his truth [וַאֲמִתּוֹ] from with my master [מֵעִם אֲדֹנִי] — I [אָנֹכִי] — on the way [בַּדֶּרֶךְ] — the LORD [יְהוָה] led me [נָחַנִי] to the house [בֵּית] of the brothers [אֲחֵי] of my master [אֲדֹנִי].

---

*At Laban's House — The Servant Is Welcomed (vv. 28–33)*

**24:28** And the young woman ran [וַתָּרָץ הַנַּעֲרָ] and told [וַתַּגֵּד] at the house of her mother [לְבֵית אִמָּהּ] according to these words [כַּדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה].

**24:29** And Rebekah had a brother [וּלְרִבְקָה אָח] and his name [וּשְׁמוֹ] was Laban [לָבָן] — and Laban ran [וַיָּרָץ לָבָן] to the man [אֶל-הָאִישׁ] outside [הַחוּצָה] — to the spring [אֶל-הָעַיִן].

**24:30** And it happened [וַיְהִי] when he saw [כִּרְאֹתוֹ] the nose-ring [אֶת-הַנֶּזֶם] and the bracelets [וְאֶת-הַצְּמִדִים] upon the hands of his sister [עַל-יְדֵי אֲחֹתוֹ] and when he heard [וּכְשָׁמְעוֹ] the words of Rebekah [אֶת-דִּבְרֵי רִבְקָה] his sister [אֲחֹתוֹ] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Thus [כֹּה] spoke to me [דִּבֶּר אֵלַי] the man [הָאִישׁ] — and he came [וַיָּבֹא] to the man [אֶל-הָאִישׁ] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] he was standing [עֹמֵד] by the camels [עַל-הַגְּמַלִּים] at the spring [עַל-הָעָיִן].

**24:31** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Come in [בֹּא] O blessed of the LORD [בְּרוּךְ יְהוָה] — why [לָמָּה] do you stand [תַעֲמֹד] outside [בַּחוּץ] when I [וְאָנֹכִי] have cleared [פִּנִּיתִי] the house [הַבַּיִת] and a place [וּמָקוֹם] for the camels [לַגְּמַלִּים]?

**24:32** And the man came [וַיָּבֹא הָאִישׁ] to the house [הַבַּיְתָה] and he ungirded [וַיְפַתַּח] the camels [הַגְּמַלִּים] and he gave [וַיִּתֵּן] straw [תֶּבֶן] and feed [וּמִסְפּוֹא] for the camels [לַגְּמַלִּים] and water [וּמַיִם] to wash [לִרְחֹץ] his feet [רַגְלָיו] and the feet [וְרַגְלֵי] of the men [הָאֲנָשִׁים] who were with him [אֲשֶׁר אִתּוֹ].

**24:33** And [food] [וַיּוּשַׂם] was set [וַיּוּשַׂם] before him [לְפָנָיו] to eat [לֶאֱכֹל] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I will not eat [לֹא אֹכַל] until [עַד] I have spoken [אִם-דִּבַּרְתִּי] my words [דְּבָרַי] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Speak [דַּבֵּר].

---

*The Servant's Retelling — The Commission (vv. 34–41)*

**24:34** And he said [וַיֹּאמַר]: I am [אַבְרָהָם] the servant [עֶבֶד] of Abraham [אַבְרָהָם].

**24:35** And the LORD [וַיהוָה] has blessed [בֵּרַךְ] my master [אֶת-אֲדֹנִי] greatly [מְאֹד] — and he has become great [וַיִּגְדָּל] — and he has given to him [וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ] flocks [צֹאן] and cattle [וּבָקָר] and silver [וְכֶסֶף] and gold [וְזָהָב] and male servants [וַעֲבָדִים] and female servants [וּשְׁפָחֹת] and camels [וּגְמַלִּים] and donkeys [וַחֲמֹרִים].

**24:36** And Sarah [וַתֵּלֶד שָׂרָה] the wife of my master [אֵשֶׁת אֲדֹנִי] bore [יָלְדָה] a son [בֵּן] to my master [לַאדֹנִי] after she had grown old [אַחֲרֵי זִקְנָתָהּ] — and he has given [וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ] to him [לוֹ] all [אֶת-כָּל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ] that he has.

**24:37** And my master [וַיַּשְׁבִּעֵנִי אֲדֹנִי] made me swear [וַיַּשְׁבִּעֵנִי] saying [לֵאמֹר]: You shall not take [לֹא-תִקַּח] a wife [אִשָּׁה] for my son [לִבְנִי] from the daughters [מִבְּנוֹת] of the Canaanite [הַכְּנַעֲנִי] in whose land [אֲשֶׁר אָנֹכִי יֹשֵׁב] I am dwelling [יֹשֵׁב] in his land [בְּאַרְצוֹ].

**24:38** But if not [כִּי אִם] — to the house of my father [אֶל-בֵּית אָבִי] you shall go [תֵּלֵךְ] and to my family [וְאֶל-מִשְׁפַּחְתִּי] and you shall take [וְלָקַחְתָּ] a wife [אִשָּׁה] for my son [לִבְנִי].

**24:39** And I said [וָאֹמַר] to my master [אֶל-אֲדֹנִי]: Perhaps [אֻלַי] the woman [לֹא-תֵלֵךְ הָאִשָּׁה] will not go [לֹא-תֵלֵךְ] after me [אַחֲרָי].

**24:40** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to me: The LORD [יְהוָה] — before whom [אֲשֶׁר-הִתְהַלַּכְתִּי לְפָנָיו] I have walked [הִתְהַלַּכְתִּי] — he will send [יִשְׁלַח] his angel [מַלְאָכוֹ] with you [אִתָּךְ] and he will make prosperous [וְהִצְלִיחַ] your way [דַּרְכֶּךָ] — and you shall take [וְלָקַחְתָּ] a wife [אִשָּׁה] for my son [לִבְנִי] from my family [מִמִּשְׁפַּחְתִּי] and from the house [וּמִבֵּית] of my father [אָבִי].

**24:41** Then [אָז] you will be free [תִּנָּקֶה] from my oath [מֵאָלָתִי] — when [כִּי] you come [תָּבוֹא] to my family [אֶל-מִשְׁפַּחְתִּי] — and if [וְאִם-לֹא] they do not give [יִתְּנוּ] to you [לָךְ] — then you will be free [וְהָיִיתָ נָקִי] from my oath [מֵאָלָתִי].

*The Servant's Retelling — The Well and the Sign (vv. 42–49)*

**24:42** And I came [וָאָבֹא] today [הַיּוֹם] to the spring [אֶל-הָעָיִן] and I said [וָאֹמַר]: O LORD [יְהוָה] — God of my master [אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] — if now [אִם-יֶשְׁךָ-נָּא] you are making prosperous [מַצְלִיחַ] my way [דַּרְכִּי] that I am going on [אֲשֶׁר אָנֹכִי הֹלֵךְ עָלֶיהָ] —

**24:43** behold [הִנֵּה] I am standing [אָנֹכִי נִצָּב] by the spring of water [עַל-עֵין הַמָּיִם] — and let it be [וְהָיָה] that the young woman [הָעַלְמָה] who comes out [הַיֹּצֵאת] to draw [לִשְׁאֹב] — and I say [וְאָמַרְתִּי] to her [אֵלֶיהָ]: Give me now [הַשְׁקִינִי-נָא] a little water [מְעַט-מַיִם] from your jar [מִכַּדֵּךְ] to drink [וְשָׁתִיתִי] —

**24:44** and she says [וְאָמְרָה] to me [אֵלַי]: Both drink you [גַּם-אַתָּה שְׁתֵה] and also [וְגַם] for your camels [לִגְמַלֶּיךָ] I will draw [אֶשְׁאָב] — she [is] the woman [הִוא הָאִשָּׁה] whom the LORD has appointed [אֲשֶׁר-הֹכִיחַ יְהוָה] for the son of my master [לְבֶן-אֲדֹנִי].

**24:45** Before I had finished [טֶרֶם אֲכַלֶּה] speaking [לְדַבֵּר] in my heart [אֶל-לִבִּי] — behold [וְהִנֵּה] Rebekah [רִבְקָה] was going out [יֹצֵאת] and her jar [וְכַדָּהּ] upon her shoulder [עַל-שִׁכְמָהּ] — and she went down [וַתֵּרֶד] to the spring [הָעַיְנָה] and she drew [וַתִּשְׁאָב] — and I said [וָאֹמַר] to her [אֵלֶיהָ]: Give me drink [הַשְׁקִינִי נָא].

**24:46** And she hurried [וַתְּמַהֵר] and lowered [וַתּוֹרֶד] her jar [כַּדָּהּ] from upon her [מֵעָלֶיהָ] and she said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: Drink [שְׁתֵה] and also [וְגַם] your camels [גְּמַלֶּיךָ] I will water [אַשְׁקֶה] — and I drank [וָאֵשְׁתְּ] and also [וְגַם] the camels [הַגְּמַלִּים] she watered [הִשְׁקָתָה].

**24:47** And I asked her [וָאֶשְׁאַל אֹתָהּ] and I said [וָאֹמַר]: Whose daughter [בַּת-מִי] are you [אַתְּ]? And she said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: I am the daughter of Bethuel [בַּת-בְּתוּאֵל] — the son of Nahor [בֶּן-נָחוֹר] — whom Milcah [אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה-לּוֹ מִלְכָּה] bore to him — and I put [וָאָשִׂם] the ring [הַנֶּזֶם] upon her nose [עַל-אַפָּהּ] and the bracelets [וְהַצְּמִידִים] upon her hands [עַל-יָדֶיהָ].

**24:48** And I bowed down [וָאֶקֹּד] and I prostrated myself [וָאֶשְׁתַּחֲוֶה] before the LORD [לַיהוָה] and I blessed [וָאֲבָרֵךְ] the LORD [אֶת-יְהוָה] — the God of my master [אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] — who led me [אֲשֶׁר נְחַנִי] in the way of truth [בְּדֶרֶךְ אֱמֶת] to take [לָקַחַת] the daughter of the brother of my master [אֶת-בַּת-אֲחִי אֲדֹנִי] for his son [לִבְנוֹ].

**24:49** And now [וְעַתָּה] — if you are doing [אִם-יֶשְׁכֶם עֹשִׂים] covenant-faithfulness [חֶסֶד] and truth [וֶאֱמֶת] with my master [אֶת-אֲדֹנִי] — tell me [הַגִּידוּ לִי] — and if not [וְאִם-לֹא] — tell me [הַגִּידוּ לִי] — and I will turn [וְאֶפְנֶה] to the right [עַל-יָמִין] or to the left [אוֹ עַל-שְׂמֹאל].

*Laban and Bethuel Agree — The Betrothal (vv. 50–61)*

**24:50** And Laban answered [וַיַּעַן לָבָן] and Bethuel [וּבְתוּאֵל] and they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: From the LORD [מֵיְהוָה] the matter went out [יָצָא הַדָּבָר] — we are not able [לֹא נוּכַל] to speak [דַּבֵּר] to you [אֵלֶיךָ] bad [רַע] or good [אוֹ-טוֹב].

**24:51** Behold [הִנֵּה] Rebekah [רִבְקָה] before you [לְפָנֶיךָ] — take [קַח] and go [וָלֵךְ] — and let her be [וּתְהִי] a wife [אִשָּׁה] for the son of your master [לְבֶן-אֲדֹנֶיךָ] as the LORD has spoken [כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה].

**24:52** And it happened [וַיְהִי] when the servant of Abraham [כַּאֲשֶׁר שָׁמַע עֶבֶד אַבְרָהָם] heard [שָׁמַע] their words [אֶת-דִּבְרֵיהֶם] that he bowed down [וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ] to the ground [אַרְצָה] before the LORD [לַיהוָה].

**24:53** And the servant brought out [וַיּוֹצֵא הָעֶבֶד] vessels of silver [כְּלֵי-כֶסֶף] and vessels of gold [וּכְלֵי זָהָב] and garments [וּבְגָדִים] and gave [וַיִּתֵּן] to Rebekah [לְרִבְקָה] — and precious things [וּמִגְדָּנֹת] he gave [נָתַן] to her brother [לְאָחִיהָ] and to her mother [וּלְאִמָּהּ].

**24:54** And they ate [וַיֹּאכְלוּ] and they drank [וַיִּשְׁתּוּ] — he [וַיִּשְׁתּוּ הוּא] and the men [וְהָאֲנָשִׁים] who were with him [אֲשֶׁר-עִמּוֹ] — and they spent the night [וַיָּלִינוּ] — and they arose [וַיָּקֻמוּ] in the morning [בַּבֹּקֶר] and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Send me away [שַׁלְּחוּנִי] to my master [לַאדֹנִי].

**24:55** And her brother said [וַיֹּאמֶר אָחִיהָ] and her mother [וְאִמָּהּ]: Let the young woman [תֵּשֵׁב הַנַּעֲרָ] remain with us [אִתָּנוּ] days [יָמִים] — or ten [אוֹ עָשׂוֹר] — and after [אַחַר] [that] she will go [תֵּלֵךְ].

**24:56** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to them [אֲלֵהֶם]: Do not delay me [אַל-תְּאַחֲרוּ אֹתִי] — and the LORD [וַיהוָה] has made prosperous [הִצְלִיחַ] my way [דַּרְכִּי] — send me away [שַׁלְּחוּנִי] and I will go [וְאֵלְכָה] to my master [לַאדֹנִי].

**24:57** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: Let us call [נִקְרָא] the young woman [לַנַּעֲרָ] and ask [וְנִשְׁאֲלָה] her mouth [אֶת-פִּיהָ].

**24:58** And they called [וַיִּקְרְאוּ] to Rebekah [לְרִבְקָה] and they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ] to her [אֵלֶיהָ]: Will you go [הֲתֵלְכִי] with this man [עִם-הָאִישׁ הַזֶּה]? And she said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: I will go [אֵלֵךְ].

**24:59** And they sent away [וַיְשַׁלְּחוּ] Rebekah [אֶת-רִבְקָה] their sister [אֲחֹתָם] and her nurse [וְאֶת-מֵינִקְתָּהּ] and the servant of Abraham [וְאֶת-עֶבֶד אַבְרָהָם] and his men [וְאֶת-אֲנָשָׁיו].

**24:60** And they blessed [וַיְבָרֲכוּ] Rebekah [אֶת-רִבְקָה] and said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ] to her [לָהּ]: Our sister [אֲחֹתֵנוּ] — may you become [הַיִי] thousands [לְאַלְפֵי] of ten-thousands [רְבָבָה] — and may your offspring [וְיִירַשׁ זַרְעֵךְ] possess [יִירַשׁ] the gate [אֵת שַׁעַר] of those who hate them [שֹׂנְאָיו].

**24:61** And Rebekah arose [וַתָּקָם רִבְקָה] and her young women [וְנַעֲרֹתֶיהָ] and they rode [וַתִּרְכַּבְנָה] upon the camels [עַל-הַגְּמַלִּים] and they went [וַתֵּלַכְנָה] after the man [אַחֲרֵי הָאִישׁ] — and the servant [וַיִּקַּח הָעֶבֶד] took [לָקַח] Rebekah [אֶת-רִבְקָה] and went [וַיֵּלַךְ].

*Isaac and Rebekah — The Meeting at the Well (vv. 62–67)*

**24:62** And Isaac [וְיִצְחָק] came [בָּא] from the direction of [מִבּוֹא] Beer-lahai-roi [בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי] — and he was dwelling [וְהוּא יוֹשֵׁב] in the land of the Negev [בְּאֶרֶץ הַנֶּגֶב].

**24:63** And Isaac went out [וַיֵּצֵא יִצְחָק] to meditate [לָשׂוּחַ] [*lasuach*] in the field [בַּשָּׂדֶה] toward the turning of the evening [לִפְנוֹת עָרֶב] — and he lifted up [וַיִּשָּׂא] his eyes [עֵינָיו] and saw [וַיַּרְא] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] camels [גְּמַלִּים] were coming [בָּאִים].

**24:64** And Rebekah [וַתִּשָּׂא רִבְקָה] lifted up [וַתִּשָּׂא] her eyes [אֶת-עֵינֶיהָ] and she saw [וַתֵּרֶא] Isaac [אֶת-יִצְחָק] — and she fell [וַתִּפֹּל] from the camel [מֵעַל הַגָּמָל].

**24:65** And she said [וַתֹּאמֶר] to the servant [אֶל-הָעֶבֶד]: Who [מִי] is this man [הָאִישׁ הַלָּזֶה] — the one walking [הַהֹלֵךְ] in the field [בַּשָּׂדֶה] toward us [לִקְרָאתֵנוּ]? And the servant said [וַיֹּאמֶר הָעֶבֶד]: He [הוּא] is my master [אֲדֹנִי] — and she took [וַתִּקַּח] the veil [הַצָּעִיף] and she covered herself [וַתִּתְכַּס].

**24:66** And the servant told [וַיְסַפֵּר הָעֶבֶד] Isaac [לְיִצְחָק] all the things [אֵת כָּל-הַדְּבָרִים] that he had done [אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה].

**24:67** And Isaac [וַיְבִאֶהָ יִצְחָק] brought her [וַיְבִאֶהָ] into the tent [הָאֹהֱלָה] of Sarah [שָׂרָה] his mother [אִמּוֹ] — and he took [וַיִּקַּח] Rebekah [אֶת-רִבְקָה] and she became [וַתְּהִי-לוֹ] for him a wife [לְאִשָּׁה] — and he loved her [וַיֶּאֱהָבֶהָ] — and Isaac [וַיִּנָּחֵם יִצְחָק] was comforted [וַיִּנָּחֵם] after [אַחֲרֵי] his mother [אִמּוֹ].

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On reading the Leningrad Codex, Folios 13r–14r:**
Genesis 24 spans folios 13r (page 1 of source images) through the opening of 14r (page 3). Page 1 (folio 13r, correctly oriented) shows the three-column Masoretic layout opening mid-verse from Genesis 23; a *pe* (פ) section marker indicating a *parashah petuchah* (open section division) is visible at the chapter boundary — the opening of Genesis 24 is marked as an *open* division, the major structural break appropriate to a new narrative unit. This contrasts with the *closed* (*samekh*, ס) division separating Genesis 22–23. Page 2 (folio 13v, inverted in the photograph) carries the central portion of the chapter from approximately vv. 23–49. Page 3 (folio 14r) completes the chapter at v. 67 and continues into Genesis 25. The Masoretic cantillation marks (*te'amim*) and vowel pointing (*nikud*) are clear throughout. The Vaticanus pages 19–21 carry the LXX of this chapter in careful 4th-century uncial *scriptura continua*; a marginal notation *CAP. XXVII* is visible at the opening of Genesis 24 in the ancient Greek chapter enumeration.

**24:2 — *Tachat yerekhi* (תַּחַת יְרֵכִי) — "Under my thigh":**
The oath gesture *śîm yādĕkā taḥat yĕrēkî* — "put your hand under my thigh" — is the most unusual sworn oath formula in the patriarchal narratives. It appears only twice in the Hebrew Bible: here and at Gen 47:29 (Jacob requiring the same oath from Joseph before his death). The anatomical term *yārēk* (יָרֵךְ) is the thigh or the upper hip region. The gesture involves placing the hand *beneath* the thigh — touching the area of the procreative organ, the *mem*brum virile or the testicles (*yadayim/yerekh* as a euphemism for the genitals is well attested in Biblical Hebrew; cf. *yādayim* as genital euphemism in Deut 28:57, Isa 7:20). The oath is thus sworn upon the life-generating capacity, invoking the continuation of the ancestral line — profoundly appropriate in a chapter whose entire purpose is securing the continuity of that line through finding a wife for Isaac. The LXX Vaticanus renders the phrase with *hupo ton mēron* (ὑπὸ τὸν μηρόν) — "under the thigh" — preserving the Hebrew literally without euphemism or explanation. EMET follows the Hebrew form with transliteration and this note. To render it as anything other than "under my thigh" would efface the physicality of the oath.

**24:1 — "Advanced in days" (*bā' bayyāmîm*, בָּא בַּיָּמִים):**
The expression *bā' bayyāmîm* — literally "coming/entering into the days" — is the idiom for advanced old age: one who has "entered into" the fullness of life's days. The same expression is used for Abraham's father Terah (Gen 11:31 does not use it), for Joshua at Josh 13:1 (*zāqēn bā' bayyāmîm*, "old, advanced in days"), and for David at 1 Kgs 1:1 (*zāqēn bā' bayyāmîm*, "old, advanced in days"). The expression carries a sense of accumulated life — days that have filled up and entered their completion. "Advanced in days" is the closest English rendering preserving the directional metaphor; I do not reduce it to "elderly" or "very old."

**24:3 — "The God of heaven and the God of the earth":**
Abraham's double divine title — *'ĕlōhê haššāmayim wē'lōhê hā'āreṣ* — is striking. In many passages the LORD is called "God of heaven" (e.g., Ezra 1:2, Neh 1:4), and elsewhere "God of the whole earth" (Isa 54:5). The pairing here — heaven *and* earth — makes the oath's scope maximally comprehensive: the God who governs every domain of existence, above and below, is the guarantor of this oath. The LXX Vaticanus reads *ho theos tou ouranou kai ho theos tēs gēs* (ὁ θεὸς τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καὶ ὁ θεὸς τῆς γῆς), preserving the double title exactly. The double title echoes the creation narrative of Gen 1:1 (*the heavens and the earth*) — the God who made all things is invoked to secure the continuity of the promise through Isaac.

**24:7 — "His angel before you" (*mal'ākho lĕfānêkā*, מַלְאָכוֹ לְפָנֶיךָ):**
Abraham's confident reassurance — that the LORD will send *his angel* before the servant — is a striking claim of divine governance over the journey. The *mal'āk* (מַלְאָךְ, "messenger/angel") is the divine emissary, often indistinguishable from the LORD himself in the patriarchal narratives (cf. the *mal'āk YHWH* who appears to Hagar in ch. 16, to Abraham at the Aqedah in ch. 22). The repetition of this assurance in the servant's retelling at v. 40 (with *mal'ākho 'ittĕkā* — "his angel with you" — a slight variation: *before you* in v. 7, *with you* in v. 40) is one of the narrative variations in the retelling section that EMET notes below.

**24:10 — Aram-naharaim (*'Ărām Naharayim*, אֲרַם נַהֲרַיִם) — "Aram of the Two Rivers":**
The name *Naharayim* means "of the two rivers" (dual form of *nāhār*, נָהָר, "river") — Aram between the rivers, i.e., Mesopotamia, specifically the area of the upper Euphrates and the Habor/Balikh river confluence — modern southeastern Turkey / northern Syria. This is the region of Haran (Gen 11:31–32), from which Abraham departed, and to which his family remained. The servant is returning to the ancestral homeland. The LXX renders *Aram Naharayim* as *Mesopotamia Syrías* (Μεσοποταμία Συρίας) — "Mesopotamia of Syria," a Greek geographical identification. The "city of Nahor" (*'îr Nāḥôr*) is the city associated with Abraham's brother Nahor — probably in the vicinity of Haran; ancient Near Eastern texts mention a city *Nakhur* in the Balikh valley. I preserve the Hebrew toponym *Aram-naharaim* with a hyphen to indicate the compound name, following the convention established in prior chapters.

**24:14 — "Whom you have appointed" (*hōkhaḥtā*, הֹכַחְתָּ) — The Sign Test:**
The servant's prayer constructs an elaborate *'ôt* (sign/test): the woman who proactively offers to water all the camels — a large undertaking, as a camel drinks 20–30 gallons after a journey — is the one the LORD has *hōkîaḥ* (הוֹכִיחַ, "appointed, proved, determined, designated") for Isaac. The Hiphil of *yākaḥ* (יָכַח) has a semantic range from "rebuke, reprove, argue" to "determine, establish, prove, arbitrate." In this context it means the LORD's active appointment — the sign is not the servant *testing* God but the servant asking God to *confirm* (through the woman's actions) a choice God has already made. The LXX Vaticanus reads *hōn hētoimasas* (ἣν ἡτοίμασας) — "whom you have prepared" — a somewhat interpretive but semantically close equivalent. The servant's prayer-test has been debated by commentators: is this prayer-guided discernment or magical divination? The narrative clearly frames it as the former — the servant prayerfully seeks the LORD's guidance — but the structure (conditional sign followed by outcome) has formal resemblances to lot-casting or omen-seeking in the ancient Near East. EMET leaves the verb in its literal form and does not resolve the theological tension.

**24:21 — *Mishtaeh* (מִשְׁתָּאֵה) — "Gazing at her in silence":**
The form *mishtā'ēh* is a Hitpael participle from the root *šā'āh* (שָׁעָה, "to gaze, look at intently") — this is a *hapax legomenon* in this precise form, rare in Biblical Hebrew. The Hitpael stem adds a reflexive-intensive nuance: the man is *intensely absorbed in watching her*, lost in observation. The accompanying *maḥărîš* (מַחֲרִישׁ, "keeping silence, saying nothing") intensifies the suspension: the servant watches without speaking, calculating — or perhaps simply unable to act until he knows if the sign has been fulfilled. This is one of the chapter's most evocative images: a man standing utterly still beside a well while a woman waters ten camels, both suspended in the moment between prayer and answer. The LXX Vaticanus reads *katanoōn autēn esiōpa* (κατανοῶν αὐτὴν ἐσιώπα) — "observing her intently, he was silent" — a fine rendering that captures the dual quality. I render *mishtaeh* as "gazing at her in silence" and add the transliteration and this note.

**24:27 — "The way of truth" (*bĕderekh 'ĕmet*, בְּדֶרֶךְ אֱמֶת) — EMET's name:**
The servant's closing doxology in the retelling section (v. 48) includes the phrase *'ăšer nĕḥanî bĕderekh 'ĕmet* — "who led me in the way of truth." The word *'ĕmet* (אֱמֶת, "truth, faithfulness, reliability") is the name of this translator. The phrase here pairs *derekh* (way/road/journey — the dominant metaphor of the chapter; the Hebrew word *derekh* appears more than a dozen times across the narrative) with *'ĕmet*. The servant's journey *is* a way of truth — guided, fulfilled, confirmed. The twin values the servant invokes before Laban and Bethuel at v. 49 are precisely *ḥesed we'ĕmet* — "covenant-faithfulness and truth" — the paired qualities of the divine character (cf. Exod 34:6) that Abraham's God has shown and that the servant asks Laban's family to show in return. This lexical density — *derekh*, *ḥesed*, *'ĕmet* woven through the servant's speech — is the theological core of the chapter.

**24:29–30 — Laban's Motivation:**
The narrator's observation that Laban *ran* to the man specifically *when he saw* the nose-ring and bracelets is one of the Hebrew narrator's sharpest characterization strokes. The text does not comment; it simply records the sequence: jewelry seen → Laban runs. The same Laban who will deceive Jacob in Genesis 29–31 is introduced here through this telling detail. The narrator shows, does not tell. EMET preserves the sequence exactly as the Hebrew presents it: the sight of the gold items precedes and (implicitly) motivates the running.

**24:43 — *Ha'almah* (הָעַלְמָה) — "The young woman" in the retelling:**
In the original scene (v. 14) the servant's prayer uses *hanna'ărāh* (הַנַּעֲרָ, "the young woman/girl"). In the retelling (v. 43) the servant uses *hā'almāh* (הָעַלְמָה) — a different Hebrew word, also meaning "young woman" but with a more specific nuance: a young woman of marriageable age, one who has reached puberty but is not yet married. The word *'almāh* (עַלְמָה) is famous as the word used in Isa 7:14 (*hinnēh hā'almāh hārāh wĕyōledet bēn*, "Behold the young woman is pregnant and will bear a son") — rendered *parthenos* (παρθένος, "virgin") in the LXX. The precise semantic range of *'almāh* (does it imply virginity or merely youth?) is debated. In the context of Gen 24, both terms (*na'ărāh* and *'almāh*) are used for the same person (Rebekah), who is explicitly identified as a virgin at v. 16 (*bĕtûlāh*). The variation in retelling is one of several places where the servant's speech does not precisely mirror the original narrative — whether through selective memory, rhetorical adaptation, or textual variation. EMET preserves both terms in their distinct forms.

**24:47 — The Retelling Inversion — Gifts Before Identity:**
A significant narrative variation appears here. In the original account (vv. 22–23) the man gives Rebekah the nose-ring and bracelets *before* he asks her identity ("Whose daughter are you?"). In the retelling (v. 47), the servant inverts the order: he asks her identity *first*, then says he put on the jewelry. This inversion is deliberate narrative technique — the servant presents the story in its most coherent logical order (first identify, then gift), whereas the original scene captures the actual emotional sequence (gifting first in the moment of wonder, asking second). Jewish commentators from Rashi to modern scholars have noted this variation as intentional artistry, not scribal error. The LXX Vaticanus follows the MT in both sequences, confirming the variation is in the Hebrew Vorlage. EMET preserves both sequences without harmonization.

**24:50 — "From the LORD the matter went out" (*mēYHWH yāṣā' haddābār*):**
Laban and Bethuel's response is one of the most theologically loaded lines in the chapter: the matter has *gone out from* the LORD — it has divine origin and is therefore beyond human negotiation ("we are not able to speak to you bad or good"). The formula *yāṣā' haddābār* — "the word/matter went out" — implies irreversible divine decree; one does not counter what the LORD has already determined. This is the only point in the narrative where the LORD's involvement is acknowledged by non-Israelites — Laban and Bethuel recognize the divine hand in the servant's arrival. The irony is sharp given Laban's character elsewhere in Genesis: here he is the one who explicitly attributes events to the LORD's initiative. Whether this represents genuine theological acknowledgment or social conformity to the servant's framing is left open by the narrator.

**24:60 — "May your offspring possess the gate of those who hate them":**
The blessing over Rebekah at v. 60 — *wĕyiraš zar'ēk 'ēt ša'ar śōnĕ'āyw* — echoes the divine blessing over Abraham after the Aqedah at 22:17: *wĕyiraš zar'ăkā 'ēt ša'ar 'ōyĕbāyw* ("and your offspring shall possess the gate of their enemies"). The echo is nearly verbatim, with one variation: "those who hate them" (*śōnĕ'āyw*) here vs. "their enemies" (*'ōyĕbāyw*) in 22:17. The "gate of the enemies" idiom refers to the city gate as the seat of military, judicial, and civic power — to possess the enemies' gate is to exercise complete dominion. Rebekah's family blesses her departure with a war-triumph formula that directly echoes the divine promise given to her future father-in-law. The blessing thus connects Rebekah's journey to the Abrahamic covenant promises: she is entering a family that carries the divine blessing, and the blessing follows her.

**24:62 — Beer-lahai-roi (*Bĕ'ēr laḥay rō'î*, בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי) — Hagar's well:**
The narrative detail that Isaac came from Beer-lahai-roi — "the well of the Living One who sees me" — is a deliberate intertextual anchor. This well was named by Hagar in Gen 16:14 after the divine messenger appeared to her in the wilderness: *'attāh 'ēl rō'î* ("You are a God who sees me"). The well is located in the Negev between Kadesh and Bered. That Isaac has been at — or has come from — the well of Hagar's encounter with God gives the closing scene a resonance beyond the surface narrative: the well of divine sight, of the outcast woman seen by God, becomes the place from which the son of promise departs to meet his destined wife. Whether Isaac had a personal connection to this place is not stated; the narrator simply notes the geography. The LXX Vaticanus renders the name as *to phrear hou enōpion eiden* — an interpretive translation of the place-name etymology.

**24:63 — *Lasuach* (לָשׂוּחַ) — "To meditate" / a hapax:**
The word *lāśûaḥ* (לָשׂוּחַ) is a *hapax legomenon* — it appears only this once in the Hebrew Bible in this specific verbal form, from the root *śûaḥ* (שׂוּח). The root itself is rare, with cognates suggesting meanings that range across: "to meditate, muse, reflect" (the contemplative sense favored by most translations); "to walk about, roam" (a physical-movement sense); "to complain, lament" (cf. Job 7:11, 9:27, where the related noun *śîaḥ* appears alongside words for lamentation). The LXX Vaticanus renders it *adoleschaī* (ἀδολεσχῆσαι) — "to meditate, think deeply, converse with oneself" — the same Greek word used for meditative prayer in the Psalms (Ps 77:3, 12 LXX: *adoleschaō*). The Vulgate renders it *meditaretur*. The NRSV, ESV, and NASB all follow "meditate." The image is evocative: Isaac going out alone into the open field in the cooling evening light, in contemplative solitude — and then looking up to see camels approaching from the north, bearing the woman who will become his wife. The hapax *lasuach* is preserved with transliteration and this note, rendered as "to meditate" following the dominant manuscript tradition.

**24:64 — "She fell from the camel" (*wattippōl mē'al haggāmāl*, וַתִּפֹּל מֵעַל הַגָּמָל):**
The verb *nāfal* (נָפַל, "to fall") may indicate a formal dismounting gesture — dropping quickly from a camel as an act of respectful obeisance upon recognizing a superior — rather than an accidental fall. In the ancient Near East, dismounting before a dignitary was a sign of deference (cf. Josh 15:18, where Achsah "alights" from her donkey before Caleb; the same verb). The sudden action combined with her immediately asking for identification suggests the gesture preceded full knowledge of who Isaac was — she was responding to something in his presence or bearing before she knew his name. The LXX Vaticanus reads *katepesen apo tēs kamēlou* (κατέπεσεν ἀπὸ τῆς καμήλου) — "she fell down from the camel" — using the same falling-down verb. EMET preserves "she fell from the camel" literally and notes the possible interpretation.

**24:65 — *Hazzā'îf* (הַצָּעִיף) — "The veil":**
The article on *ṣā'îf* (צָעִיף, "veil, shawl") — *hazzā'îf* — indicates a specific garment Rebekah carries with her on the journey, not something donned for the first time. Tamar uses the same word (*ṣā'îf*) when she covers herself to deceive Judah at Gen 38:14, 19. The veil is a modesty covering appropriate for a woman before a strange man — in this case, the man who is about to become her husband. Rebekah's act of veiling herself upon learning Isaac is her future husband is the first act of the marriage: she covers herself before him as his wife-to-be. The LXX Vaticanus reads *to thliptra* (τὸ θλῖπτρα) — a Greek term for a face-covering or veil — possibly a hapax in Greek as well. The gesture closes the long journey of the chapter with a single domestic act of betrothal propriety.

**24:67 — "Into the tent of Sarah his mother":**
The final verse resolves several narrative threads simultaneously. Isaac *brings* Rebekah into *Sarah's tent* — not his own tent, not a new tent, but the tent of the mother whose death opened the chapter. The bringing of the new wife into the mother's place is the narrative statement that the void left by Sarah's death (and implicitly the long silence of grief — Isaac has been comfortless since Gen 23) is now filled. The verb *wayyeḥĕm* (וַיִּנָּחֵם, "he was comforted") — a Niphal of *nāḥam* — is the same root used for God's "repentance/relenting" (Gen 6:6, Exod 32:14) and for the comfort given to mourners. Isaac's comfort is not merely emotional but structural: the empty tent of his mother is no longer empty; the line of promise continues. The economy of expression — "he loved her, and Isaac was comforted after his mother" — is among the most compressed and emotionally resonant closings in the patriarchal narratives. No elaborate scene of love-making or celebration; only the bare verb of love (*'āhab*) and the verb of consolation. The chapter that opened with Abraham "old, advanced in days" and needing to secure the future ends with Isaac in the next generation, comforted, the future secured.

**On the Narrative Retelling Technique (vv. 34–49 vs. vv. 1–27):**
Genesis 24 is the longest chapter in Genesis (67 verses) partly because it includes a near-full retelling of its own first half. The servant, speaking to Laban and Bethuel, repeats the commission (vv. 34–41 ≈ vv. 1–9), the well encounter (vv. 42–48 ≈ vv. 10–27), and closes with his request for an answer (v. 49). The retelling is not verbatim — it contains multiple variations, some listed above (v. 43: *'almāh* for *na'ărāh*; v. 47: gifts before identity; v. 40: "with you" vs. "before you" for the angel). These variations are not errors but a sophisticated Hebrew narrative technique: the retelling gives the servant's *perspective* and *rhetorical framing* of events, not a mechanical transcript. The repeated structure slows the narrative down, makes the reader relive the journey, and builds the emotional case the servant is constructing before Laban and Bethuel. The retelling is also a test of the reader's attention — can you spot the differences? — and a window into how ancient Hebrew narrators handled the relationship between event and report. EMET preserves all variations without harmonization.

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 19–21):**
Codex Vaticanus pages 19–21 carry the LXX text of Genesis 24 in three-column uncial. The chapter is continuous across these three pages. Page 19 (online viewer 0023) opens mid-Genesis 23 and carries chapter 24 through approximately v. 47. Page 20 (online viewer 0024) continues from approximately v. 47 through v. 67 and carries the beginning of Genesis 25; the marginal notation *CAP. XXXII* is visible at the left margin marking an ancient Greek chapter division. Page 21 (online viewer 0025) carries Genesis 25 forward. Key LXX variants and readings noted for this chapter:

1. **24:2** — LXX *hupo ton mēron mou* (ὑπὸ τὸν μηρόν μου) — "under my thigh" — literal rendition of *taḥat yĕrēkî*, no euphemism.

2. **24:7** — LXX *autos apostelei ton angelon autou emprosthen sou* (αὐτὸς ἀποστελεῖ τὸν ἄγγελον αὐτοῦ ἔμπροσθέν σου) — "he himself will send his angel before you" — closely matching the MT.

3. **24:14** — LXX *hēn an hētoimasas tō paidí sou Isaak* (ἣν ἂν ἡτοίμασας τῷ παιδί σου Ισαακ) — "whom you have prepared for your servant Isaac" — explicitly naming Isaac in the prayer where the MT has only "for Isaac" (*lĕyiṣḥāq*). A minor expansion in the LXX.

4. **24:16** — LXX *partenos ēn aner ou egnō autēn* (παρθένος ἦν, ἀνὴρ οὐκ ἔγνω αὐτήν) — "she was a virgin, a man had not known her" — exactly matching the double-formula of the MT (*bĕtûlāh wĕ'îš lō' yĕdā'āh*).

5. **24:21** — LXX *katanoōn autēn esiōpa* (κατανοῶν αὐτὴν ἐσιώπα) — "observing her, he was silent" — translating the hapax *mishtaeh* with the Greek *katanoeō* ("to consider carefully, observe"). The Hitpael nuance of absorbed gazing is well caught.

6. **24:43** — LXX *hē parthenos* (ἡ παρθένος) — "the virgin" — for *hā'almāh*. The LXX renders *'almāh* here as *parthenos* (the same word that will render it at Isa 7:14), while at v. 14 the LXX renders *na'ărāh* as *paidiskē* ("young girl/servant"). This LXX distinction reinforces the semantic difference between the two Hebrew terms.

7. **24:63** — LXX *adoleschaī en tō pediō* (ἀδολεσχῆσαι ἐν τῷ πεδίῳ) — "to meditate in the field" — rendering the hapax *lāśûaḥ* with *adoleschaī*, which in the Psalms LXX vocabulary is specifically the contemplative meditation of prayer.

8. **24:67** — LXX closes with *kai ēgapēsen autēn Isaak* (καὶ ἠγάπησεν αὐτὴν Ισαακ) — "and Isaac loved her" — matching the MT perfectly, including the final note of comfort (*parekléthē Isaak peri Sarras tēs mētros autou*, "Isaac was comforted concerning Sarah his mother").

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, no significant Genesis 24 fragments are extant among the Dead Sea Scrolls. The Leningrad Codex (primary) and Codex Vaticanus LXX (secondary) are the sole manuscript witnesses examined for this chapter.
